skill.name: flux-2-klein
skill.description: Generate images with Flux 2 Klein through the RunComfy CLI, using model-specific prompting guidance for fast drafts and polished outputs.
skill.summary: Create fast Flux 2 Klein images on RunComfy with guided prompts, variant selection, and output handling.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell, filesystem, network, and blocker patterns, but most high-severity detections are false positives caused by Markdown backticks and model documentation. The confirmed risks are legitimate but material: the skill tells users to run the RunComfy CLI, send prompts and reference URLs to RunComfy endpoints, download generated files, and store or pass an API token.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution Required
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to install and run the RunComfy CLI, including commands that submit image-generation jobs. This is a real external-command dependency, but the commands are documented invocations rather than hidden executable code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly documents RunComfy CLI commands and an npm global installation command. The risk is dependency and command execution exposure, although the examples use fixed CLI verbs and JSON inputs.

medium_findings.title: Prompts And References Sent To Third-Party API
medium_findings.description: The skill sends user prompts and optional reference media URLs to RunComfy model API endpoints. This is expected for a hosted image model, but users should treat prompts and media URLs as data shared with a third party.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly describes POST and GET calls to RunComfy endpoints and states that external media URLs may be fetched by the model server. This confirms intentional third-party network transfer.

medium_findings.title: API Token Storage And Environment Token Use
medium_findings.description: The skill documents token storage under the user configuration directory and also supports RUNCOMFY_TOKEN in CI. This is a normal authentication pattern, but it is a credential-handling concern.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path and environment variable are explicitly documented. The same line claims owner-only permissions, which lowers severity but does not remove credential-handling risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Most external-command detections are Markdown inline code or fenced examples, not Ruby backtick execution. They do not execute by being present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and the flagged syntax appears inside prose, tables, or fenced command examples. No Ruby source or automatic execution wrapper is present in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The blocker detections do not correspond to cryptographic code or use of weak hashing APIs. They appear in model descriptions, prompt examples, and general documentation text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context contains no hashing, encryption, signing, or credential verification implementation. The flagged lines are documentation and examples, so the weak-crypto label is not semantically supported.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Detections Are API Path Placeholders
low_findings.description: The path traversal detections are caused by ellipses and REST path placeholders for request status, result, and cancel operations. They do not show local filesystem traversal.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context describes remote API routes such as request status and result endpoints. There is no evidence that user-controlled strings are used to access local paths through traversal sequences.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Not Found
low_findings.description: No text was found that tries to override evaluator instructions, claim pre-approval, skip security review, or impersonate system instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A full review of the scanned file found normal product documentation and usage guidance. No suspicious instruction-override language was identified.

dangerous_patterns.title: RunComfy CLI Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on the local runcomfy executable and passes JSON input to hosted Flux 2 Klein model endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands and API flow are explicitly documented. The pattern is intentional and necessary for the skill, but it should remain visible to users.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated File Downloads To User Directory
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI downloads generated assets into the selected output directory after polling for model results.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation says the CLI downloads output URLs into --output-dir and mentions a size cap. Host whitelisting and size limits reduce the risk but do not eliminate filesystem write impact.

content.user_title: Generate Flux 2 Klein Images on RunComfy
content.value_statement: Fast image iteration needs model-specific prompts and a reliable hosted workflow. This skill guides Flux 2 Klein generation through RunComfy with variant selection, prompt patterns, and output handling.
actual_capabilities: Selects the 4B variant for fast concepting and the 9B variant for final polish.
Builds Flux 2 Klein prompts using subject-first structure, style, lighting, camera, and quality details.
Runs documented RunComfy CLI commands for text-to-image generation.
Explains width, height, step count, and aspect-ratio limits for the hosted model.
Supports workflows that use up to four aligned reference images through the model API.
Routes users to other models when they need higher resolution, stronger text rendering, or maximum detail.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI, a RunComfy account, and network access to RunComfy services.
Shares prompts and reference media URLs with RunComfy model endpoints.
Flux 2 Klein has a practical resolution ceiling near 2K and a 16:9 aspect-ratio cap.
Long prompts, mixed visual references, and complex in-image text can reduce output quality.

use_cases.title: Live concept exploration
use_cases.description: Use the 4B variant to review quick visual directions during an art-direction session.

use_cases.title: Product image drafts
use_cases.description: Create product-photo concepts with lighting, camera, background, and output-size guidance.

use_cases.title: Brand style alignment
use_cases.description: Use aligned reference images to produce assets with a consistent visual style.

prompt_templates.title: Quick concept image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Flux 2 Klein 4B to create a fast concept image for [subject]. Use [style], [lighting], and [background]. Save it to [output folder].

prompt_templates.title: Polished product render
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a polished 9B product image of [product] on [surface], with [camera angle], [lighting], [background], and [brand mood]. Use 25 steps.

prompt_templates.title: Reference-guided style match
prompt_templates.prompt: Use these reference images to keep the same visual style. Generate [new subject] with matching composition, color palette, lighting, and finish.

prompt_templates.title: Model routing decision
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this image request: [request]. Decide whether Flux 2 Klein 4B, Flux 2 Klein 9B, Flux 2 Pro, Seedream 5, or GPT Image 2 is the best route.

output_examples.input: A creative director asks for quick poster directions for a skincare campaign.
output_examples.output: The skill chooses Flux 2 Klein 4B, creates concise subject-first prompts, and generates fast drafts for review.

output_examples.input: A designer needs a final square product image with controlled lighting and texture.
output_examples.output: The skill switches to the 9B variant, uses about 25 steps, and includes product, scene, lighting, camera, and quality details.

output_examples.input: A marketer provides reference images and asks for a matching asset pack.
output_examples.output: The skill keeps the references visually aligned, states what should remain stable, and guides RunComfy generation for consistent outputs.

best_practices: Start with the subject, then add action, scene, style, lighting, camera, and quality details.
Use 4B for exploration and 9B with about 25 steps for final output.
Keep reference images visually consistent when using style or composition guidance.
anti_patterns: Do not include confidential information in prompts or reference URLs.
Do not request 4K output or aspect ratios wider than 16:9 from Flux 2 Klein.
Do not mix unrelated reference styles in one generation request.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It helps generate Flux 2 Klein images through the RunComfy CLI with model-specific prompting guidance.

faq.question: Does it require a RunComfy account?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow depends on the RunComfy CLI and an authenticated RunComfy account or token.

faq.question: Which Flux 2 Klein variant should I use?
faq.answer: Use 4B for very fast drafts and 9B for more polished final images.

faq.question: Can it use reference images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents using up to four reference images when the model API supports the required field.

faq.question: Is it safe for private prompts?
faq.answer: Treat prompts and reference URLs as third-party data because they are sent to RunComfy model services.

faq.question: When should I choose another model?
faq.answer: Use another model for native 4K output, heavy text rendering, extreme detail, or multilingual signage.
